competition.narrative: Competitive landscape is partially known. Microsoft Dynamics 365 is the incumbent ERP and is not an S2P competitor; it is the integration target and a decision criterion (NPCI uses D365 for PR/PO and requires Zycus to integrate with it). NPCI is dissatisfied with D365 for end-to-end procurement (per 29 Jun call: 'Dissatisfaction with the relevant stakeholder 365 for end-to-end procurement (Dynamics used only for PR/PO; sourcing, RFQ, approvals, FA notes handled offline)'), which is a positive signal for Zycus. However, the buyer's statement that they will 'float a limited RFP next week' (per 29 Jun call) implies a multi-vendor shortlist, but no other S2P competitors were named on the 29 Jun discovery call. Stealth risk: a 'limited RFP' often means a pre-qualified shortlist of 2-4 vendors, and we do not know who else is invited. Likely competitors (inferred, not confirmed): D365-native or Microsoft-partnered S2P vendors (e.g. a procurement module/add-on from a Microsoft ISV partner that claims seamless D365 integration), or established S2P players in the APAC/India market (e.g. SAP Ariba, Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, or a regional player). NPCI's emphasis on D365 integration capability (the buyer explicitly asked 'Have you done anywhere the D365 integration?' on 29 Jun, the first competitive-positioning question on the call) signals this will be the key differentiator, and any competitor with a strong D365 story will be a high threat. How we win: lead with D365 integration proof (demo seamless PR/PO/invoice sync with D365 and provide a named D365 customer reference), differentiate on agentic AI (NPCI explicitly requested AI agents for procurement, per 29 Jun call, and no mainstream S2P competitor has true agentic AI in production as of mid-2026), and position intake management from the relevant stakeholder as a zero-change-management win (NPCI uses Teams internally, per 29 Jun call). Risk: if Zycus cannot demo D365 integration credibly or provide a D365 reference, NPCI may favor a D365-native or Microsoft-partnered competitor even if that competitor is weaker on AI. Next move: during the demo (act by 8 Jul 2026), ask NPCI directly who else they are evaluating, and lead the demo with D365 integration proof and a D365 customer reference (if available).

identify_pain.narrative: NPCI's operational pain is clear and well-articulated on the 29 Jun discovery call. The pain owner is Pallavi Sonawane (Information Technology Procurement, Decision Maker) and Aishwarya Shirgaonkar (IT Procurement Associate, Decision Maker), who led the 29 Jun call and described the current manual procurement burden. Confirmed pain points: (1) IT procurement is 60-80% manual (per buyer statement on 29 Jun call: 'NPCI IT procurement is highly manual (est. 60–80% manual)'), creating high labor cost and error risk. (2) Invoicing is entirely manual, with 8-10 staff handling ~200 PO invoices/month (per 29 Jun call: 'current invoicing team of 8–10 handles ~200 invoices/month manually; PO invoices only'), and non-PO invoices are not yet scoped. (3) Supplier onboarding is manual and email-based, with no automated document validation (per 29 Jun call: 'replace manual email onboarding and manual document checks'). (4) Ad hoc procurement requests via email/Teams/WhatsApp cause lost SLAs and manual follow-up overhead (per 29 Jun call: 'current ad hoc requests via email/Teams/WhatsApp cause lost SLAs and manual follow-ups'). (5) No central auditability or repository for RFQs, approvals, FA notes, and redlines; today these are Word files with physical/manual signatures, then duplicated into D365 (per 29 Jun call: 'today FA notes and approvals are Word files with physical/manual signatures and then duplicated into D365'). (6) Dissatisfaction with the relevant stakeholder 365 for end-to-end procurement (per 29 Jun call: 'Dissatisfaction with the relevant stakeholder 365 for end-to-end procurement (Dynamics used only for PR/PO; sourcing, RFQ, approvals, FA notes handled offline)'). (7) No supplier performance monitoring or utilization tracking (per 29 Jun call: 'currently manual/no utilization tracking'). Inferred pain: NPCI is a national payments infrastructure organization and likely faces regulatory/audit scrutiny, making the lack of auditability and the manual/offline approval process a compliance risk (not explicitly stated on the call, but implied by the buyer's emphasis on 'auditability and central repository'). The pain is owned by procurement (Pallavi, Aishwarya) but the financial impact (labor cost of 8-10 invoicing staff, SLA failures, compliance risk) likely escalates to finance/CFO, though the CFO has not yet been engaged.

decision_process.narrative: NPCI is running a compressed, budgeted RFP process with a stated vendor finalization target of end Jul 2026 (~4 weeks from the 29 Jun discovery call). The process timeline per the 29 Jun call: (1) Discovery call with procurement (Pallavi Sonawane, Aishwarya S, Hilary Fonseca) completed 29 Jun 2026. (2) NPCI will float a 'limited RFP' next week (early Jul 2026, per buyer statement on 29 Jun call: 'NPCI will float a limited RFP next week'). (3) Vendor finalization by end Jul 2026 (per buyer statement on 29 Jun: 'expects to finalize vendor by end of July'). (4) Follow-up demo requested by NPCI (portal demo, with NPCI solutions team + Zycus IT to participate, per 29 Jun call: 'Schedule follow-up demo of Zycus platform (portal demo requested by NPCI; solutions team + Zycus IT to participate)'). The 'limited RFP' phrasing implies either a sole-source or a targeted shortlist (not a broad open RFP), but we do not yet know if this is a multi-vendor bake-off or how many competitors are invited. Approval chain: the buyer confirmed the project is budgeted (per 29 Jun call), meaning senior finance/C-level approval has already been obtained for the procurement, but we do not know who signed the budget or how many layers of approval are required for vendor selection and contracting. The compressed 4-week timeline (discovery to vendor finalization) is unusually fast for an enterprise S2P deal and leaves minimal room for champion-building, EB mapping, or recovery from a weak demo/RFP response. The deal owner must execute the demo and RFP response in the next 7-14 days (by 8-15 Jul 2026) to stay in the race.

decision_criteria.narrative: NPCI's evaluation criteria are clear from the 29 Jun discovery call and map to their stated S2P pain points and RFP intent. Confirmed criteria: (1) Microsoft Dynamics 365 integration capability (the buyer explicitly asked 'Have you done anywhere the D365 integration?' on 29 Jun, signaling this is a critical decision gate; PRs originate in D365 and Zycus must sync requisitions, PO approvals, and push PO/invoice data back to D365). (2) Sourcing automation (eRFX, supplier shortlist, bid collection, automated scoring) to replace manual RFQ handling. (3) Supplier onboarding and vendor self-service portal (to replace manual email onboarding and document validation). (4) eInvoicing with OCR and 2-way/3-way matching (to reduce the 8-10 invoicing headcount burden and integrate invoice flow into D365). (5) Contract Lifecycle Management (CLM) and auditability (today FA notes and approvals are Word files with physical/manual signatures, then duplicated into D365; buyer wants a central repository for RFQs, approvals, FA notes, redlines). (6) Agentic AI capabilities for procurement and intake management (buyer explicitly requested AI agents and expressed strong interest in AI for procurement on 29 Jun: 'Strong interest in AI for procurement and invoicing; NPCI explicitly wants AI agents for procurement'). (7) Microsoft Teams integration for intake management (to consolidate ad hoc requests from email/Teams/WhatsApp). Inferred criteria: supplier performance monitoring and early risk detection (buyer was asked about top 2-3 critical suppliers for performance tracking and expressed interest). The RFP is 'limited' (per 29 Jun call), implying NPCI may already have a shortlist or is running a targeted multi-vendor evaluation; we do not yet know the RFP scoring rubric or relative weighting of D365 integration vs AI vs price.

deltas.subject: Economic Buyer (likely CFO or Finance Controller) is unmapped and unengaged. The 29 Jun discovery call was procurement-only (Pallavi Sonawane, Aishwarya S, Hilary Fonseca), with no finance or C-level participation. The buyer confirmed the project is budgeted (per 29 Jun call: 'Project is budgeted'), meaning budget approval exists at a senior level, but we have no access to that level and do not know who signed the budget. Without EB access, Zycus is being sized and scoped by mid-level procurement with no business-case champion at the decision table when the RFP shortlist is evaluated. The compressed timeline (vendor finalization end Jul, ~4 weeks) leaves minimal time to map and engage the EB. Mitigation: ask Pallavi or Aishwarya directly who controls the budget and commercial approval, and request an intro before the demo (act by 8 Jul 2026).

deltas.to: Differentiate on agentic AI (the wedge NPCI explicitly requested). NPCI stated 'Strong interest in AI for procurement' and 'explicitly wants AI agents for procu
deltas.subject: Differentiate on agentic AI (the wedge NPCI explicitly requested). NPCI stated 'Strong interest in AI for procurement' and 'explicitly wants AI agents for procurement' on the 29 Jun call. Make Zycus's Merlin/I2O agentic capabilities (autonomous event creation, bid analysis, negotiation assist, intake) the centerpiece of the demo and RFP response. No mainstream S2P competitor has true agentic AI in production as of mid-2026, so this is the highest-leverage differentiation vs the stealth shortlist. Act by 8 Jul 2026 (demo) and 15 Jul 2026 (RFP response).
